channel.title: Feature Requests (new posts)
channel.description: Posts in the forum category "Feature Requests" - What features or capabilities would you like to see in the next version of the Foundation website?

item.title: Advanced Rank System: Re: Advanced Rank System
item.content:encoded: The issue WJ-132 calls for listing staff information in some official manner, similar to the Staff Identification userscript that Croquembouche mentioned. I agree some kind of staff rank specification outside of moderator/admin is also desirable.

item.title: Advanced Rank System: Re: Advanced Rank System
item.content:encoded:
We have a Staff Identification userscript available, which shows users' staff ranks beneath their name on forum posts, so you can get the feature you want right now. But that's an ugly workaround and yes, we definitely want this to be built-in to PF.

I don't think we want to be able to have completely custom ranks - we don't want this to end up like a Minecraft server where everyone has a vanity rank. But a site will have full control over what ranks it creates, and it won't be limited to just Moderator and Admin like we are currently.

item.title: Advanced Rank System: Re: Advanced Rank System
item.content:encoded: We probably won't replace karma if only because I don't wanna hear the complaints. But the ability to add per-site titles/ranks is still viable anyway.

item.title: Advanced Rank System: Advanced Rank System
item.content:encoded:
Description: Replace Karma with a rank system that reflects your function on the wiki. The system would be managed by administrators and would allow for the creation of custom ranks.

Reason: The karma system is quite useless and random, I think that a system that would allow to know what are the prerogatives of a person we talk to on the site (thanks to an icon for example) would be more useful.

Advantages: Know a user/staff member's function more clearly.

Disadvantages: For the moment I do not find any disadvantages.

item.title: Better curation/discovery tools: Re: Better curation/discovery tools
item.content:encoded: This is an ongoing process amongst staff- a major effort is currently in the works on this wiki, and we will be continuing to explore options.

item.title: Better curation/discovery tools: Better curation/discovery tools
item.content:encoded:
Description: New or expanded ways to curate and find content. I don't have a specific suggestion just now, but I know there have been some threads over the years (e.g., in proposals and policy) where recommendations have just fallen through because wikidot couldn't handle them.

This might involve looking at other communities and organizations for curation methods, making it easy for readers to categorize or reccomend articles, or some kind of more complex technical approach.

(Side thought, it would also help to interview some representative users to identify their needs and current search techniques so that you could build on those.)

At the very least, you could add code features wikidot doesn't have so that people can add these later.

Reason: Wikiwalking and finding good content is the core activity for readers, and for some articles like tales discoverability is seriously lacking.

Advantages: ^

Disadvantages: I guess it would take some effort to identify and implement the best techniques.

item.title: Scpper-like features: Re: Scpper-like features
item.content:encoded: Man oh man do I want this. SCPPER is such a great site and if I could access its info on the main site it’d be so much more convenient. It’s also very helpful for authors to keep track of their own articles with SCPPER and if we could have it all on one site I’m sure it would be very beneficial to writers.

item.title: Scpper-like features: Scpper-like features
item.content:encoded:
Description: Scpper-like stats & features built into the wiki.

Reason: Scpper provides a ton of useful features (searchable by keyword or author, tracks stats over time, archives deleted articles) for both tracking individual articles and wiki-wide functionally.

Advantages: Makes stats-nerds (users and staff) happy, helpful with discovery.

Also (and I mean absolutely no shade to FiftyNine here, they are wonderful), integrating it and hosting it on the same servers improves access to the tools and could lead to less chug.

Disadvantages: Possibly difficult integration, don't want to steal FiftyNine's thunder.

item.title: Word Count: Re: Word Count
item.content:encoded:
I don't really see a downside to this. It might be a bit hard to work out how to calculate e.g. do you include words inside CSS blocks? But it's definitely a net benefit overall. You could even expose the same metric to ListPages, provided you could guarantee it's calculated the same way client-side and server-side.

Highlighting certain parts of text gives you word count for that part.

I do feel like this extends beyond what the site should do, though.

item.title: Word Count: Word Count
item.content:encoded:
Description: Pretty simple idea, simply add a word count box below text boxes for purposes of knowing. Highlighting certain parts of text gives you word count for that part.

Reason: Quite a dumb one really, but when revising my concept, I keep forgetting to check word count. It would probably be helpful to just have a box for words. A small idea mainly for convenience, but a welcome one.

Advantages: No need to check word count using foreign website, ease of access and remembering.

Disadvantages: Not really, unless you wanna count the tiny bit of extra space it takes.

item.title: Retain the RSS feed system: Re: Retain the RSS feed system
item.content:encoded: There's no plans to axe anything there, but there will be enhancements around notifications in general such that RSS will be one option among several.

item.title: Retain the RSS feed system: Retain the RSS feed system
item.content:encoded: I currently use the RSS Feeds associated with the "new posts" link on the discussion threads for my SCPs. It would be nice if these RSS Feeds were still available in the next version of the website.

item.title: Keeping the look of the website consistent: Re: Keeping the look of the website consistent
item.content:encoded:
I think the author is referring to inconsistent application of BHL, which debuted in 2019.

I can't say I know what the "third one" is, though.

item.title: Keeping the look of the website consistent: Re: Keeping the look of the website consistent
item.content:encoded:
Recently, the SCP Foundation has changed the sites feel and look

I don't know that I'd consider early 2014 as recently, if I'm being honest.

item.title: Keeping the look of the website consistent: Technical Co-Captain/Admin Post
item.content:encoded:
There are no plans to disallow custom CSS on the EN wiki at this time.

We consider themes to be creative works, and have specific policies around them. "Immersion" is not a primary concern compared to creative freedom.

item.title: Keeping the look of the website consistent: Re: Keeping the look of the website consistent
item.content:encoded:
This website, despite what it may desire you to believe, is not an actual secret database of anomalous creatures, locales, or other phenomenon that exists for real in the world today. It is instead a creative writing project, with many hundreds of contributors and thousands of pages. There are so many stories to tell here, and stories that have been told - stories about the Foundation, the people who live within its circles, those who live without, and everything in between.

Because of this, and because the act of crafting fiction is inherently a creative endeavour, it should surprise nobody that there are those among us who sometimes feel like the scope of the stories we're trying to tell goes outside the limitations of the default experience. Having a default theme is important because it provides an aesthetically unobtrusive backdrop for anyone who has not yet or does not care to tell their stories with their styling as well as their words. However, I can tell you for a fact that I would not have been able to express what I wanted to express with something like SCP-5935 had it not been for that freedom to change the display of the wiki to fit the story being told.

I know this is an unpopular opinion in some circles, especially those who would prefer to maintain the pristine aesthetic of the wiki and keep it in an unblemished state. They are certainly free to do whatever they wish and run their wikis however they want, but that decision comes with the cost of creative freedom. Consistency is limitation, and just like all books shouldn't have the same cover, not all stories can be told with the same style choices. The EN wiki has been exceptionally good about fostering an environment where its authors can tell the stories they desire to tell, free from lesser restrictions, and beholden only to the rating module.

I feel that some users' issues stem from their desire for every article to be the same, because it doesn't make sense that a database would have different styling for different pages. I implore these users to remember that this isn't a government database or secret codex - it's a creative fiction website. Whether you like it or not, the authors here are artists, and the new page screen is their canvas. I, for one, would be severely disappointed to see the freedom of stylistic expression taken off the writers' palette.

item.title: Keeping the look of the website consistent: Re: Keeping the look of the website consistent
item.content:encoded:
There's arguments for and against allowing custom CSS on the site. Our EN branch is very permissive about it. Other international branches are less permissive, some banning Black Highlighter, some allowing CSS only if it's relevant to the article and even then it needs to be an established GoI theme.

Either way, it's not a discussion for Project Foundation, but rather a discussion for site staff now. There will almost certainly be a way to include CSS on a page. Whether or not that's allowed is a different question, which doesn't need Project Foundation to be answered.

I think it's most likely that, when we transition, the look and feel of the site will remain the same. Whether or not that includes BHL and other CSS themes will be something for us to decide in the meantime.

item.title: Keeping the look of the website consistent: Re: Keeping the look of the website consistent
item.content:encoded:
I think that you are talking about themes, and there are a lot more than three of them.

The option to use various themes to fit the canon or feel that an author is going for in their piece is a major part of the creative process. There are lots of skips and tales that utilize specific themes in order to showcase their work in ways they find enjoyable. Removing this function from the site would be detrimental to hundreds of pre-existing skips. This would create untold hours of labor for the staff to identify each page that is missing a theme component, then figuring out how to display those components using some sort of "standard" theme.

This would make the site exceptionally non-functional, and I absolutely am opposed to the idea.

item.title: Keeping the look of the website consistent: Keeping the look of the website consistent
item.content:encoded:
Recently, the SCP Foundation has changed the sites feel and look

Now, this would be fine if the changes had been throughout the site, but other parts of the site including the front page stick to the original feel, and there is even a third one, unrelated to the new one and old one.

All I ask is that once we move away from Wikidot that we try to keep the feel of the site consistent.

item.title: Notification System Addition.: Re: Notification System Addition.
item.content:encoded: Good. When it does get added, it'll be a lot easier for people trying to get their concepts greenlighted or get critique.
